card.title: Mitochondrial Eve: Your Oldest Mother
card.teaser: Every human alive shares one mother — a single woman who lived in Africa about 155,000 years ago. And we can prove it.
card.coverImagePrompt: A silhouette of a woman standing on a savanna hill at dusk, glowing DNA helices rising from her like smoke, painterly, symbolic, warm tones

lessons.title: A woman we all remember
lessons.body: Trace your mother's mother's mother, far enough back, and you'll meet her. So will everyone else on Earth. She isn't Adam-and-Eve mythology — she's a real person, genetically locatable, who lived in East Africa during a cooling climate about 155,000 years ago. Her name, because we had to call her something, is Mitochondrial Eve.

visual.imagePrompt: A young woman with braided hair looking at sunrise over a dry East African landscape, documentary-style lighting

lessons.title: The gift only mothers give
lessons.body: Inside every one of your cells are hundreds of mitochondria — tiny power plants with their own small ring of DNA. Crucially, you inherited all of them from your mother. Sperm mitochondria are destroyed at fertilization. That makes mitochondrial DNA a pure maternal line, unbroken from mother to daughter across generations.

keyTerms.definition: Cellular structures that generate energy and carry their own DNA
Mitochondrial DNA, inherited exclusively from the mother

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic illustration of a mitochondrion with a small circular DNA molecule inside, glowing subtly

lessons.title: Coalescence, not creation
lessons.body: Mitochondrial Eve wasn't the first woman, nor the only woman alive at her time. She's simply the most recent common ancestor in an unbroken female line. Other women lived alongside her — but at some point, every one of their maternal lines had only sons, and the chain snapped. Hers kept going. Through sheer demographic luck, we all inherited her mitochondria.

visual.imagePrompt: A branching family tree diagram where most lineages fade out and only one vertical line continues to the present, elegant infographic

lessons.title: The molecular clock
lessons.body: In 1987, researchers at Berkeley sampled mtDNA from 147 people worldwide and tallied the mutations. Because mtDNA mutates at a roughly steady rate, they could read the differences like tree rings. The result: all modern mtDNA converges on a single woman in Africa. The finding held up across thousands of later samples, refined to roughly 100,000–200,000 years ago.
lessons.funFact: If Mitochondrial Eve met Y-chromosomal Adam, their lifetimes wouldn't have overlapped by tens of thousands of years. They're ancestors of everyone, but not of each other.

visual.imagePrompt: A laboratory scene from the 1980s with DNA sequencing gels on a lightbox, researcher in a white coat, vintage color grade

lessons.title: We are one family, stubbornly
lessons.body: Every human being shares a great-great-...-grandmother less than 10,000 generations back. On genealogical scales, that's nothing. The lesson of Mitochondrial Eve isn't just biology — it's that the 'deep' differences between us are dwarfed by a very recent, very African, very shared origin.

quiz.question: Who do you inherit your mitochondrial DNA from?
quiz.explanation: Sperm mitochondria are destroyed at fertilization, so mtDNA comes entirely from the mother.

quiz.question: Was Mitochondrial Eve the only woman alive in her time?
options: Yes, she was the first human
No, many women lived alongside her
quiz.explanation: Many women lived at the same time; only her unbroken female lineage survived to the present.

quiz.question: Roughly when did Mitochondrial Eve live?
quiz.explanation: Genetic analyses converge on roughly 100,000–200,000 years ago, with a best estimate near 155,000.

sources.title: A revised root for the human Y chromosomal phylogenetic tree
